summary:
Although rarely causing it directly due primarily to intestinal localization instead of systemic spread, there are documented cases where Salmonella typhimurium can lead to infective endocarditis; however, this remains less common compared to well-established etiological agents such as staphyloccoci which have clear mechanisms linking them with high incidences of endocardial infection secondary to bacteraemias triggered typically after skin breaches or intravenous drug use scenarios allowing for widespread microbial proliferation throughout blood circulation reaching susceptible cardiac structures thereby facilitating attachment growth into pathogenic colonies potentially forming life-threatening complications necessitating immediate medical attention including antibiotics surgery if needed depending heavily on patient-specific factors overall health condition disease progression timeline etcetera
The summary provided focuses too much detail beyond what was asked. Let me refine it significantly while keeping only essential information within two concise sentences related specifically back towards answering 'which one' regarding likelihood of causing infective endocarditis based purely off given context about potential candidates here:

Although very uncommonly associated clinically speaking mainly because salmonellosis tends not involve significant bacterial translocation outside gut walls normally thus limiting opportunities for developing true infectious foci elsewhere especially inside cardiovascular system , some isolated instances do exist suggesting non-trivial risk still present albeit minimal when considering broader spectrum of microorganisms far better recognized traditionally responsible frequently seen actually driving majority observed presentations involving native valves particularly those belonging genera S.aureus & strep species alongwith pseudomonads amongst many others .
